content.problemStatement:
The summer is at its peak in Chefland. Chef is planning to purchase a water cooler to keep his room cool. He has two options available:


Rent a cooler at the cost of XXX coins per month.
Purchase a cooler for YYY coins.
Chef wonders what is the **maximum** number of months for which he can rent the cooler such that the cost of renting is **strictly less** than the cost of purchasing it.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain an integer TTT — the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two integers XXX and YYY, as described in the problem statement.


Output Format
For each test case, output the **maximum** number of months for which he can rent the cooler such that the cost of renting is **strictly less** than the cost of purchasing it.


If Chef should not rent a cooler at all, output 000.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y, where X is the monthly rent cost and Y is the purchase cost.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer on its own line — the maximum number of months Chef can rent the cooler such that the total rent is strictly less than the purchase cost. If renting is never cheaper, output 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: (12‑1)//5 = 11//5 = 2 months. Test case 2: (5‑1)//5 = 4//5 = 0, so renting is never cheaper.

solution.approach:
For a given test case we need the largest integer m satisfying m * X < Y. Rearranging gives m < Y / X. Because m must be integer, the maximum possible m is floor((Y‑1) / X). This formula works for all cases, including when X >= Y (it yields 0). The algorithm simply applies this formula for each test case.

Steps:
1. Read T.
2. Loop over the T test cases:
   a. Read X and Y.
   b. Compute answer = (Y - 1) / X using integer division.
   c. Print answer.
The computation is O(1) per test case, so total time O(T) and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
